preFix.title: Applying a Venetian plaster finish to a wall
preFix.subtitle: Build up layered trowelled plaster to a polished marble-like surface
preFix.excerpt: How to apply a Venetian plaster (stucco lustro) finish to an interior wall, covering the two or three trowelled layers of lime-based plaster, the burnishing technique that produces the polished surface, and the clear wax protection coat. Covers colour mixing and troubleshooting imperfections.
preFix.sourceNotes: Historic England Technical Advice Note on lime plaster (open-access). Milner's Practical Plastering (1896). City and Guilds Level 3 Plastering (polished plaster unit). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: finish rewards patience and practice. The first wall attempt rarely produces a result that matches the reference images; the technique needs to be felt as much as described, and the

stage in particular has a narrow timing window. Practice the trowel sweeps on a piece of board or a small section of wall before tackling a full room. This is a technique for people who are comfortable with a plastering trowel; the level above standard skimming.

Venetian plaster compound (lime-based or acrylic-lime blend, sold pre-mixed; buy enough for two to three times the wall area as wastage is high in learning). Universal tint or pigment paste for colouring. A clean steel plastering trowel kept scrupulously clean (any dried plaster on the face will score the finish). Liming wax or clear carnauba paste wax for the final protection coat. Lint-free cloths for wax application.

The substrate must be smooth, dry, and sound. Sand any ridges, fill any cracks, and apply a thin bonding primer (usually supplied or specified by the Venetian plaster manufacturer) to improve adhesion on new plaster or bare plasterboard. Allow the primer to cure fully. Remove dust, as any grit on the surface will score the trowel face during burnishing.
Step 2, first coat, the base layer

Mix the plaster compound with the chosen tint to a consistent colour throughout. Load a small charge onto the trowel and apply in wide, diagonal sweeping strokes, holding the trowel at approximately 15° to the wall. Each sweep should be 500 to 800 mm long; overlap the edges of each sweep by 50 mm. Leave deliberate voids and skip marks between sweeps; these expose the wall colour beneath and create the layered depth characteristic of the finish. Work in sections of approximately 1 m² before the coat dries. Allow to dry fully, 2 to 4 hours.
Step 3, second coat, build and vary

Apply the second coat in the same sweeping diagonal strokes, crossing the direction of the first coat. The second coat fills the voids from the first coat partially but deliberately leaves some first-coat colour visible. The aesthetic goal is a layered, slightly translucent look with variation across the surface. Use a slightly lighter or slightly darker version of the mixed colour for the second coat if you want to increase depth. Allow to dry.

The burnishing window opens when the second coat has dried to a surface-dry state but is still cool to the back of the hand, typically 20 to 40 minutes after application depending on room temperature. Press the clean trowel flat against the surface with firm, even pressure and rub in tight circular and figure-of-eight strokes. The trowel generates friction heat; the heat and pressure compress and polish the plaster particles. Work in sections of 300 × 300 mm, overlapping each pass. The surface will change from matt to satin to gloss as the burnishing progresses; stop when the finish reaches the level of sheen desired.

Apply a thin coat of clear paste wax with a lint-free cloth in circular motions, allow to haze, then buff with a dry cloth. Waxing protects the surface from moisture and everyday scuffing. Re-wax annually in bathrooms or areas where condensation is likely.

attrs.fix: Clean the trowel face completely before burnishing and check it again every few passes. Lightly re-wet and re-trowel scored areas. Keep a clean damp cloth at hand to wipe the trowel between passes.
attrs.cause: Dried plaster particles on the trowel face; any grit or dried material scores the soft surface during burnishing.
attrs.symptom: Score marks visible in the finished surface

attrs.fix: Lightly mist the dry surface with water and allow to penetrate for 2 to 3 minutes to re-open the burnishing window, then burnish again. If this fails, apply a thin skim of fresh compound and burnish that.
attrs.cause: Burnishing started too late; the plaster had dried beyond the window where burnishing is effective.

attrs.fix: Mix the compound more thoroughly before the next coat, stirring to the bottom of the container. A third, thin unifying coat applied in long, even strokes with a uniform mix will reduce the variation.
attrs.cause: Inconsistent tint mixing; the colour was not mixed evenly through the compound before application.
attrs.symptom: Uneven depth of colour across the wall

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
